summary: I was at the Tule Lake War Relocation Center as a young child during World War II. Tule Lake was known for having a section for 'no-no boys' who were actually jailed there, but my family was in the family section. I remember the strict routines, like bowing to the east and singing the Japanese national anthem during undokai. There were also difficult moments, like the boiler room fire and the harsh living conditions with one-room houses and outdoor latrines. Despite the hardships, I have vivid memories of daily life and the community there.

Pearl Harbor was the event that changed everything for my family. After the attack on December 7, 1941, my dad said we voluntarily evacuated. My family left Berkeley and went to Auburn, California, hoping to be safe in what was called the 'white zone.' This event led to our eventual relocation to the camps, including Tule Lake. Pearl Harbor was the catalyst for all the upheaval we experienced during the war.

After Pearl Harbor, my family voluntarily evacuated to Auburn, California, because it was designated a 'white zone' and thought to be safer. We stayed on a farm outside Auburn in a tool shed and a tent because my mother was pregnant with my sister. Auburn was a temporary stop before we were rounded up and sent to the camps. It was a difficult time, but my mother managed to keep her sewing machine with her, which became her lifeline.

Before the war, my family lived in Berkeley, California, where my parents worked at North Bay Cleaners on Vine Street. Berkeley was home before we evacuated to Auburn and later were sent to the camps. After the war, we intended to return to Berkeley, where my mother's oldest brother, Frank Matsui, was settled and helping set things up. Berkeley was the place we considered home before and hoped to return to after the war.

I learned that some Japanese families, including a friend’s family, started a community in Utah during the war and never went to the camps. This was different from my family’s experience, as we were sent to Tule Lake. The Utah community was organized like a 'Noah’s Ark,' selecting people with different skills to survive. It was interesting to hear that not all Japanese Americans were interned, and some found ways to avoid the camps by relocating to places like Utah.

I first heard about Heart Mountain Relocation Center when I was getting my credential at CAL, long after the war. It was one of the camps in Wyoming, where some Japanese Americans were interned. I was raised in Wyoming after the war, so I became aware of the camp’s history later in life. Heart Mountain was part of the broader story of Japanese American incarceration during World War II.

Before the war, my parents worked at North Bay Cleaners on Vine Street in Berkeley. My dad worked there before they married, and my mom did mending and sewing buttons. It was their livelihood before the upheaval caused by the war and the forced relocation. North Bay Cleaners represents the normal life they had before everything changed.

Frank Matsui was my mother’s oldest brother who was settled in Berkeley before and after the war. After we were released from the camps, Frank was helping to set things up for our family’s return to Berkeley. He was an important family connection during the difficult transition back to civilian life. Frank’s presence gave us hope for rebuilding our lives after the war.

After the war, my family stayed in Mount Shasta for about a year because things were still tense about Japanese Americans returning. My dad worked on the railroad there, and we lived in a section house near the train tracks with other Japanese families. The house was basic, with bunk beds and a potbelly stove, and the trains passing by made it feel like the place was tipping over at night. Mount Shasta was a transitional place where we tried to rebuild our lives before moving back to Berkeley.

My dad worked for the Southern Pacific Transportation Company after we left the camps, specifically while we stayed in Mount Shasta. He worked on the railroad, living in a section house that was usually for migrant workers. This job was important for our family’s survival during the difficult post-war period when returning to Berkeley was not yet possible. Southern Pacific gave my dad a way to provide for us in uncertain times.

I met my spouse at Berkeley schools, and we both ended up working at the University of California, Berkeley. My mother’s friend, Eleanor Sweat, was connected to UC Berkeley through her husband, who taught there. I also attended CAL for my credential program, where I first learned about the internment camps. Berkeley has been a significant place in my life for education, work, and reconnecting with my roots after the war.

I heard about the 'no-no boys' when I was older and started learning about the camps. I thought my dad was a no-no boy because we were at Tule Lake, which had a section for them, but he said that wasn’t true. The no-no boys were people who answered 'no' to loyalty questions and were sometimes jailed at Tule Lake. It was a confusing and complex part of the camp experience that I only understood later in life.

At Tule Lake, I remember having to bow to the east and sing the Japanese national anthem during the undokai, which was a military-like exercise. I was just a preschooler and didn’t understand what was going on, but the instructors were strict and disciplined. This was part of the pro-Japanese schooling that took place in the camps before the government found out and deported the teachers. The anthem and the rituals were confusing for a child caught between two worlds.
